topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
researcher_bg: HCI/visualization researcher studying explainable AI for virtual cell models and gene prediction workflows
expert_bg: Molecular biologist conducting gene perturbation experiments and evaluating virtual cell model reliability

shared_root.label: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
shared_root.description: Interview topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

exp_L1_00.label: Model Biological Fidelity
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether virtual cell models accurately reflect real biological systems and mechanisms.

exp_L2_00_00.label: Mechanistic Plausibility
exp_L2_00_00.description: Whether the model's internal logic aligns with known molecular pathways and regulatory logic.

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Black-box predictions contradict established pathway logic
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: AI outputs suggest gene perturbation effects that violate well-documented signaling cascades or feedback loops, raising doubts about interpretability and trust.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Inability to capture non-linear epistatic interactions
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: Models often assume additive or linear gene effects, failing to represent complex genetic interactions observed in wet-lab experiments.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: Intuitive sense that the model treats the cell as a homogeneous soup, neglecting subcellular localization critical for gene function.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Contextual Specificity
exp_L2_00_01.description: Models fail to account for cell-type, tissue, or condition-specific biology.

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: Predictions don't generalize across cell lines despite similar genotypes
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: Model trained on one cancer line fails dramatically on another with minor epigenetic differences, limiting experimental utility.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Can't distinguish between transient vs. stable knockdown effects
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Model conflates acute CRISPRi effects with long-term knockout adaptations, leading to misleading design of follow-up assays.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: Gut feeling that the model assumes standard culture conditions, ignoring how nutrient availability alters gene essentiality.

exp_L1_01.label: Experimental Validation Burden
exp_L1_01.description: Challenges in translating model predictions into reliable, testable wet-lab hypotheses without excessive validation overhead.

exp_L2_01_00.label: Prediction Ambiguity
exp_L2_01_00.description: Uncertainty in what exactly the model is predicting (e.g., phenotype, expression change, viability) and at what resolution.

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Outputs lack clear mapping to measurable phenotypes
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: Model returns abstract 'perturbation scores' that don’t correspond directly to RNA-seq fold-changes, protein levels, or growth rates.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Uncertainty estimates are uncalibrated
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: Reported confidence intervals don’t match empirical error rates observed during validation experiments.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: Model predictions appear suspiciously continuous; real biology often shows threshold effects or bimodal responses that feel missing.

exp_L2_01_01.label: Resource Misalignment
exp_L2_01_01.description: Mismatch between model requirements and practical lab constraints (time, cost, reagents).

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: Top predictions require hard-to-engineer combinatorial perturbations
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: Model suggests triple-knockouts that are technically infeasible or prohibitively expensive to validate.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Prioritization ignores assay compatibility
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: Model ranks genes that can’t be assayed with available readouts (e.g., no antibody, no reporter).

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: Intuitive frustration that the model generates hundreds of high-confidence hits, but we can only test ~20 per month.

exp_L1_02.label: Data and Training Limitations
exp_L1_02.description: Issues stemming from the quality, scope, and biases in training data used to build virtual cell models.

exp_L2_02_00.label: Coverage Gaps
exp_L2_02_00.description: Training data lacks representation of key biological scenarios or gene classes.

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: Poor performance on non-coding RNAs and poorly annotated genes
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: Model heavily biased toward well-studied protein-coding genes; novel lncRNAs yield unreliable predictions.

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Underrepresentation of stress-response conditions
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: Most training data comes from homeostatic conditions; predictions under hypoxia, drug treatment, or differentiation are weak.

exp_leaf_02_00_02.label: The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects
exp_leaf_02_00_02.description: Tacit knowledge that alternative splicing matters, but model treats genes as monolithic units—feels biologically naive.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Noise and Batch Artifacts
exp_L2_02_01.description: Training data contains technical artifacts that mislead model learning.

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: Batch effects from different CRISPR libraries propagate into predictions
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Model learns library-specific guide efficiencies rather than true biological effects.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: Off-target effects in training data create false dependencies
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: Perturbations appear to affect unrelated pathways due to uncorrected off-target activity in source datasets.

exp_leaf_02_01_02.label: Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean
exp_leaf_02_01_02.description: Training data shows unrealistically low noise in control conditions, making the model overconfident in subtle effects.

exp_L1_03.label: Interpretability and Trust
exp_L1_03.description: Difficulty understanding why the model makes certain predictions, hindering scientific insight and adoption.

exp_L2_03_00.label: Attribution Opacity
exp_L2_03_00.description: Inability to trace which features or pathways drive a prediction.

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Feature importance maps don’t align with known regulators
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: SHAP or saliency maps highlight irrelevant genes while ignoring master regulators validated in literature.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: No mechanistic story behind counterintuitive predictions
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: When the model predicts synthetic lethality between two unrelated genes, there’s no biological narrative to guide hypothesis generation.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: I can’t tell if it’s learned biology or dataset quirks
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: Persistent doubt that patterns reflect real mechanisms versus idiosyncrasies of the training corpus (e.g., publication bias).

exp_L2_03_01.label: Reproducibility Concerns
exp_L2_03_01.description: Model behavior changes unpredictably across versions or inputs, eroding confidence.

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Minor input formatting changes cause large output swings
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: Switching gene ID formats (Ensembl vs. HGNC) leads to inconsistent predictions for the same target.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Model updates break previously validated predictions
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: A gene pair confirmed experimentally as non-interacting becomes a top hit after a model retraining.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: Intuition that the model performs well only on genes or conditions very close to its training set, lacking true abstraction.

shared_root.label: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
shared_root.description: Interview topic: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

res_L1_00.label: Model Interpretability & Trust
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts assess, interpret, and build trust in AI-driven virtual cell models like CellForge.

res_L2_00_00.label: Reliability Assessment Criteria
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts judge whether a virtual cell model is trustworthy or reliable.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Determine which quantitative or qualitative metrics (e.g., prediction accuracy, biological plausibility) experts rely on when assessing virtual cell models.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Understand role of domain knowledge in validation
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Explore how experts integrate prior biological knowledge to validate or reject model outputs.

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Assess confidence interpretation needs
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Investigate how experts want to understand the model’s confidence under gene perturbations and whether current representations suffice.

res_L2_00_01.label: Explainability Needs for Causal Reasoning
res_L2_00_01.description: What kinds of explanations help experts reason about causal mechanisms in gene perturbation outcomes.

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Clarify desired causal insights from perturbations
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Determine whether experts seek mechanistic explanations (e.g., why perturbing Gene A affects Gene B) versus correlative patterns.

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Map explanation types to biological questions
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Link specific explanation formats (e.g., pathway diagrams, influence graphs) to common research goals like drug target discovery.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Assess whether standard XAI techniques (e.g., SHAP, attention maps) meet the explanatory depth required for gene regulatory reasoning.

res_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration & Usability
res_L1_01.description: How virtual cell AI tools fit into existing experimental and analytical workflows of genetic researchers.

res_L2_01_00.label: Current Perturbation Experiment Workflow
res_L2_01_00.description: Characterize manual or traditional workflows for gene perturbation to identify integration points for AI tools.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Document end-to-end manual perturbation workflow
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Capture steps, tools, and decision points in non-AI-based gene perturbation experiments to inform tool design.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Identify pain points in current workflows
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Uncover bottlenecks, redundancies, or high-effort stages where AI assistance could add value.

res_leaf_01_00_02.label: Determine data inputs and outputs in practice
res_leaf_01_00_02.description: Understand what data formats, scales, and metadata are typically used before and after perturbation experiments.

res_L2_01_01.label: AI Tool Adoption Barriers
res_L2_01_01.description: Explore challenges that prevent or complicate adoption of AI-based gene prediction models.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Assess familiarity and prior experience with AI models
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Gauge baseline exposure to AI in genomics to tailor onboarding and interface complexity.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Identify usability obstacles in existing AI tools
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Discover interface, documentation, or interaction issues that hinder effective use of gene prediction models.

res_leaf_01_01_02.label: Understand integration requirements with lab systems
res_leaf_01_01_02.description: Determine compatibility needs with existing bioinformatics pipelines, databases, or wet-lab protocols.

res_L1_02.label: Knowledge Discovery & Scientific Insight
res_L1_02.description: How experts aim to generate novel biological hypotheses or insights using virtual cell models.

res_L2_02_00.label: Targeted Biological Questions
res_L2_02_00.description: What specific scientific problems experts hope to solve using virtual perturbation models.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Elicit whether goals include identifying driver genes, predicting phenotypic outcomes, or discovering therapeutic targets.

res_leaf_02_00_01.label: Prioritize types of actionable insights
res_leaf_02_00_01.description: Determine which outputs (e.g., ranked gene lists, pathway disruptions, trait associations) are most valuable for downstream validation.

res_leaf_02_00_02.label: Assess need for comparative perturbation analysis
res_leaf_02_00_02.description: Explore whether experts want to compare multiple perturbations (e.g., single vs. combinatorial knockouts) within the same interface.

res_L2_02_01.label: Supporting Evidence & Contextualization
res_L2_02_01.description: What external knowledge sources experts need to contextualize and validate AI predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify preferred evidence types for validation
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine whether literature, pathway databases, or prior experimental data are most trusted for verifying predictions.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Evaluate integration of literature with model output
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Assess how automatically linking predictions to relevant publications could enhance understanding or trust.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Understand role of community knowledge
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Explore whether expert consensus, annotations, or shared interpretations could supplement model explanations.

res_L1_03.label: User Experience & Interface Design
res_L1_03.description: Design considerations for interfaces that support effective interaction with virtual cell AI models.

res_L2_03_00.label: Explanation Presentation Preferences
res_L2_03_00.description: How experts prefer to receive and interact with model explanations.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Determine optimal explanation modalities
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Assess preference for visual (e.g., graphs, heatmaps), textual (e.g., natural language), or interactive (e.g., sliders, what-if) explanations.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Evaluate granularity control needs
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Investigate whether users want to drill down from high-level predictions to molecular-level mechanisms.

res_leaf_03_00_02.label: Assess real-time interactivity requirements
res_leaf_03_00_02.description: Determine if immediate feedback during perturbation parameter adjustment is critical for hypothesis testing.

res_L2_03_01.label: Cognitive & Perceptual Load Management
res_L2_03_01.description: How to minimize cognitive burden when interpreting complex gene interaction predictions.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Identify sources of cognitive overload
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Pinpoint aspects of current or hypothetical interfaces that overwhelm users (e.g., too many genes, unclear causality).

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Explore scaffolding strategies for novices
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Determine if guided workflows or tooltips could help less-experienced users engage with the model effectively.

res_leaf_03_01_02.label: Assess need for customizable views
res_leaf_03_01_02.description: Understand whether experts want to tailor visual encodings or layout based on their subfield (e.g., cancer vs. developmental biology).

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Inability to capture non-linear epistatic interactions
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

conflict.expert_branch: Inability to capture non-linear epistatic interactions
conflict.researcher_branch: Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on the inability of current models to capture non-linear epistatic interactions is highly relevant—it directly impacts the biological realism and predictive reliability of virtual cell simulations in gene perturbation studies.

pivot.limitation: Without first clarifying the overarching research objectives for using CellForge, efforts to improve mechanistic plausibility may misalign with actual scientific priorities, leading to over-engineering fidelity in areas that don’t advance core discovery goals.
pivot.research_goal: Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge
pivot.compelling_reason: By explicitly mapping user intent—such as identifying driver genes or therapeutic targets—you ensure that model enhancements like epistasis modeling are strategically directed toward questions that matter most, maximizing both scientific impact and model utility.
pivot.coarse_scenario: A team begins their morning by aligning their CellForge experiments with clearly defined objectives like target discovery, allowing them to prioritize which genetic interactions truly need high-fidelity modeling. This focus prevents wasted effort and accelerates the path from simulation to actionable biological insight.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Understand role of domain knowledge in validation

conflict.expert_branch: Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization
conflict.researcher_branch: Understand role of domain knowledge in validation

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to missing contextual cues from imaging data or from literature-based localization annotations?
When evaluating model outputs, which heuristic dominates: known co-localization rules or expected functional compartmentalization?
Does your intuition rely more on canonical localization patterns (e.g., nuclear = transcription factors) or cell-type-specific deviations?
Is your concern driven more by physical proximity requirements for protein interactions or by shared pathway membership regardless of location?
choices: Shared pathway membership regardless of location

probes.question: Which matters more: absolute expression levels in a compartment or relative enrichment compared to other compartments?
Is your judgment more sensitive to spatial co-occurrence or temporal co-activation of gene products?
Does your concern stem more from geometric constraints (e.g., organelle size/shape) or from biochemical microenvironments?
Is your intuition more influenced by known diffusion barriers (e.g., nuclear envelope) or by active transport mechanisms?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the model incorporated compartment-specific expression thresholds derived from single-cell spatial transcriptomics instead of bulk RNA-seq, would your judgment about 'Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization' change?

If protein interaction predictions were constrained by known physical proximity within organelles rather than genome-wide co-expression, would your judgment about 'Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization' change?

If the model accounted for diffusion limitations across the nuclear envelope but ignored mitochondrial membrane barriers, would your judgment about 'Feels like the model ignores spatial compartmentalization' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify preferred evidence types for validation

conflict.expert_branch: Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify preferred evidence types for validation

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to visible culture conditions (e.g., color, turbidity) or invisible metabolic states (e.g., redox potential, ATP levels)?
Which trade-off weighs more heavily in your intuition: false positives in gene essentiality calls or missed context-dependent essentials?
choices: False positives in gene essentiality calls

probes.question: Does your unease arise more when nutrients are severely limited or when they are abundant but imbalanced?
Is your concern stronger when growth rates are near zero or when they are suboptimal but nonzero?
Which media factor most triggers your suspicion: carbon source variability or trace element omission?
Do you find gene essentiality shifts more predictable across strain backgrounds or across nutrient environments?
When evaluating metabolic assumptions, do you prioritize central carbon flux rerouting or cofactor availability constraints?
Has your gut feeling been reinforced more by failed replications in your lab or by inconsistent results across published studies?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the carbon source in the culture medium changed from glucose to acetate, would your judgment about 'Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions' change?
If the growth rate was experimentally clamped to 0.1 h⁻¹ regardless of nutrient composition, would your judgment about 'Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions' change?

If prior experimental data showed consistent gene essentiality across five different minimal media formulations, would your judgment about 'Something feels off about the metabolic state assumptions' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify sources of cognitive overload

attributes: Presence of Known Thresholds or Switches
Historical Precedent from Literature or Past Experiments

probes.question: Is your concern more about the smoothness of predictions in low-data regions or in high-data regions?
When evaluating model outputs, do you rely more on known biological thresholds or on statistical confidence intervals?
Which matters more to your distrust: the absence of sharp transitions or the lack of bimodal output distributions?

probes.attribute: Presence of Known Thresholds or Switches
probes.question: Is your skepticism stronger when the model ignores a well-established activation threshold or when it smooths over a known bistable switch?
choices: Smooths over a known bistable switch

probes.question: Does your concern increase more when biological noise is heteroscedastic or when it exhibits bursty/spike-like behavior?

probes.attribute: Historical Precedent from Literature or Past Experiments
probes.question: Is your intuition driven more by patterns observed in your own lab data or by consensus findings in published literature?
choices: Patterns in your own lab data

probes.question: Would you trust the edge cases more if the model used interpretable rules (e.g., decision trees) or if it matched known pathway logic?
Is the smoothness problem worse when inputs are aggregated gene sets or when they are individual gene expression levels?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the model’s prediction curve included a sharp transition at a known biological threshold (e.g., p53 activation at 2.5-fold DNA damage), would your judgment about 'I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth' change?
If input features were reduced from 10,000 genes to only 5 core pathway genes with known switch-like behavior, would your judgment about 'I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth' change?

If the training data explicitly included synthetic examples of bimodal responses based on literature-reported bistability, would your judgment about 'I just don’t trust the edge cases—it’s too smooth' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Clarify desired causal insights from perturbations

conflict.expert_branch: The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects
conflict.researcher_branch: Clarify desired causal insights from perturbations

probes.question: Is your concern primarily about the input data representing genes as single units rather than distinguishing isoforms?
choices: Yes, the data lacks isoform-level resolution
No, the data is sufficient but the model interpretation is flawed

probes.question: When evaluating model predictions, do you rely more on known isoform-specific functional annotations or on aggregate gene-level behavior?
Which level of biological representation is more critical for accurate mechanistic insight: gene-level or isoform-level?
Does your concern assume that isoforms are functionally non-redundant, or could redundancy explain why gene-level modeling sometimes suffices?
choices: Isoforms are typically non-redundant in function
Functional redundancy often makes gene-level modeling acceptable

probes.question: Is your concern stronger in contexts where isoform expression varies significantly across tissues or conditions?
choices: No, the issue persists regardless of expression context

probes.question: When designing perturbations, do you aim to target specific isoforms or the entire gene locus?
Do you expect the model to incorporate curated knowledge about isoform functions, or infer relationships purely from data?
choices: Infer purely from data without prior knowledge

probes.question: Is your concern driven more by whether isoform-specific effects alter observable phenotypes or by theoretical biological fidelity?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the model used isoform-resolved RNA-seq data instead of gene-aggregated counts, would your judgment about 'The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects' change?
If all isoforms of a gene were functionally redundant in the system under study, would your judgment about 'The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects' change?
If perturbations were designed to selectively knock down individual isoforms rather than the whole gene, would your judgment about 'The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

probes.question: Is your concern about the negative controls being 'too clean' more related to statistical distribution shape or absolute magnitude of observed values?
Which contextual cue matters more when you sense something is off: the lab protocol used or the cell type being modeled?
Does your intuition about unrealistic cleanliness depend more on expected technical noise or expected biological noise in controls?
When judging if negative controls are suspiciously clean, do you rely more on known pathway variability or population-level heterogeneity?
Is your concern more likely triggered by deviations in sample preparation steps or measurement instrument settings?
Which historical reference matters more: your own past experiments or published literature baselines?
Do you assess 'too clean' controls based more on the ratio of treatment effect size to control variance or on the absolute control variance alone?
choices: Ratio of treatment effect size to control variance

probes.question: Is your suspicion of overly clean controls more influenced by within-batch consistency or between-batch uniformity?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the observed standard deviation in negative controls decreased from 0.15 to 0.02 while all other experimental conditions remained identical, would your judgment about 'Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean' change?

If the cell type changed from primary human fibroblasts (known for high donor variability) to an immortalized cell line with low baseline heterogeneity, would your judgment about 'Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean' change?

If the measurement platform switched from a high-noise legacy instrument to a newly calibrated ultra-sensitive detector, would your judgment about 'Something’s wrong with the negative controls—they’re too clean' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

conflict.expert_branch: It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify metrics used to evaluate model reliability

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to the sparsity of gene expression data or the presence of batch effects in the training set?
Which matters more when judging generalization: consistent directional predictions across similar conditions or high point-wise accuracy on held-out samples?
Does your sense of instability arise more from test conditions that are out-of-distribution in gene space or in perturbation type?
When you feel the model is memorizing, is it because outputs violate known pathway logic or because they lack mechanistic interpretability?
Is your concern stronger when small input noise causes large output swings or when identical inputs yield variable outputs across runs?
choices: Small input noise → large output swings

probes.question: Do you distrust the model more when it’s overconfident on rare genes or underconfident on common ones?
Is memorization more likely in your view when the training set lacks diversity in cell states or in genetic backgrounds?
Which signals poor generalization more clearly: inconsistent predictions between sister cell lines or erratic behavior in evolutionarily distant types?
choices: Inconsistent predictions between sister cell lines
Erratic behavior in evolutionarily distant types

hypothetical_scenarios: If the training data included 10× more diverse genetic perturbations but kept the same number of cell types, would your judgment about 'It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing' change?

If the model’s predictions became perfectly calibrated (confidence matches empirical accuracy) but biological plausibility remained low, would your judgment about 'It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing' change?

If test conditions were restricted to only those within the convex hull of training gene expression profiles, would your judgment about 'It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Black-box predictions contradict established pathway logic
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Clarify desired causal insights from perturbations

conflict.expert_branch: Black-box predictions contradict established pathway logic
conflict.researcher_branch: Clarify desired causal insights from perturbations

analogy.source_concept: Inability to capture non-linear epistatic interactions

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene perturbation data (e.g., single and double knockouts) that exhibit context-dependent effects
structural_mapping.logic: Model assumes linear or additive gene effects, failing to represent higher-order genetic dependencies that alter expected outcomes
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that contradict observed epistasis in wet-lab experiments, suggesting implausible biological behavior

analogy.explanation: Just as the model’s failure to encode non-linear epistasis leads to biologically implausible predictions, the researcher’s focus on clarifying desired causal insights addresses whether the model should explain *why* a perturbation has a specific effect (e.g., through pathway logic) rather than just predicting it. Both issues stem from a mismatch between the model’s internal assumptions and the expert’s expectation of mechanistic consistency—whether in genetic interaction structure or signaling causality.

scenario.inputs: Perturb PTEN (a tumor suppressor) in a virtual cell model trained on cancer cell line data; the model predicts upregulation of AKT activity, which aligns with known PI3K-AKT pathway logic, but also predicts downregulation of mTORC1—a direct downstream effector of AKT—which contradicts established signaling hierarchy.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a structured query: 'Do you need an explanation of how PTEN loss propagates through PI3K→AKT→mTORC1, or are you only interested in the statistical association between PTEN perturbation and mTORC1 levels?' This clarifies whether the model must embed mechanistic constraints or can rely on correlative patterns.
scenario.edge_cases: When the model correctly predicts mTORC1 downregulation due to compensatory feedback (e.g., via S6K-IRS1 loop) not captured in canonical pathway diagrams, the expert may still reject the output as 'contradicting pathway logic,' while the researcher might accept it if the goal is predictive accuracy over textbook mechanism—highlighting their divergent priorities.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Predictions don't generalize across cell lines despite similar genotypes
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify usability obstacles in existing AI tools

conflict.expert_branch: Predictions don't generalize across cell lines despite similar genotypes
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify usability obstacles in existing AI tools

analogy.source_concept: Can't distinguish between transient vs. stable knockdown effects

structural_mapping.inputs: Perturbation data from short-term CRISPRi experiments (expert's frame) ↔ User interaction logs, error reports, or workflow traces from biologists using the AI tool (researcher's frame)
structural_mapping.logic: Model conflates distinct biological states due to insufficient temporal resolution ↔ Researcher analyzes usage patterns but fails to differentiate between user confusion caused by poor documentation vs. unintuitive interface design
structural_mapping.outputs: Misleading assay designs based on incorrect assumptions about gene function ↔ Misdiagnosed usability issues that lead to ineffective UI changes or irrelevant documentation updates

analogy.explanation: Just as the expert’s model misattributes phenotypic outcomes because it doesn’t account for the duration of genetic perturbation, the researcher risks misidentifying the root cause of adoption barriers if they don’t disentangle different types of user friction—such as whether a biologist abandons a tool because they can’t find instructions (documentation issue) or because the input format doesn’t match their experimental workflow (interface issue). In both cases, surface-level similarity masks critical underlying distinctions that determine downstream validity.

scenario.inputs: A computational biologist uploads a gene knockout list from a pancreatic cancer line (e.g., PANC-1) into CellForge, expecting predictions for a follow-up screen in a genetically similar line (e.g., BxPC-3), but the interface requires manual re-specification of cell-type metadata and offers no warning about cross-line generalization limits.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s usability study identifies that users consistently skip the metadata step due to unclear labeling, leading to silent failures; they recommend inline validation tooltips and automatic genotype-aware warnings when inputs mismatch training contexts.
scenario.edge_cases: When the two cell lines share >95% genomic identity but differ in KRAS activation state due to culture conditions, the expert expects the model to fail biologically—but the researcher observes that users blame the tool’s ‘unreliability’ rather than contextual limitations, revealing a gap: the expert sees a fidelity problem, while the researcher sees a communication/design failure that obscures the model’s actual scope.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Can't distinguish between transient vs. stable knockdown effects
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

conflict.expert_branch: Can't distinguish between transient vs. stable knockdown effects
conflict.researcher_branch: Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

analogy.source_concept: Predictions don't generalize across cell lines despite similar genotypes

structural_mapping.inputs: Perturbation data from a specific experimental context (e.g., acute CRISPRi in one cell line)
structural_mapping.logic: Model assumes biological effects are invariant across temporal or contextual shifts, just as it assumes invariance across genetically similar but epigenetically distinct cell lines
structural_mapping.outputs: Misleading predictions that conflate fundamentally different biological states (transient vs. stable knockdown, or Line A vs. Line B)

analogy.explanation: Just as the model fails to account for how subtle epigenetic differences between cell lines drastically alter gene essentiality—leading to poor generalization—it similarly fails to differentiate between transient and stable knockdowns because it treats all perturbation data as if drawn from a single, static biological context. In both cases, the model’s core assumption of context-invariance causes misalignment with real-world biology.

scenario.inputs: A researcher inputs a request to use CellForge to identify synthetic lethal partners for BRCA1 loss, specifying they intend to validate hits using both short-term siRNA knockdown and long-term CRISPR knockout in MCF10A cells.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s approach would produce a prioritized list of candidate genes, annotated with whether each prediction is expected to hold under transient or stable perturbation conditions—enabling assay design aligned with biological timescales.
scenario.edge_cases: If the model internally conflates transient and stable effects (as the expert notes), it might rank a gene as high-confidence based on acute CRISPRi data, but that gene could be compensated over time in a knockout setting—leading the researcher to waste resources validating a false positive that only appears essential in short-term assays.

Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

conflict.expert_branch: Prioritization ignores assay compatibility
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

analogy.source_concept: Top predictions require hard-to-engineer combinatorial perturbations

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene perturbation candidates generated by the AI model (e.g., triple-knockouts)
structural_mapping.logic: Model prioritizes based on internal scoring without checking experimental feasibility constraints
structural_mapping.outputs: High-confidence predictions that cannot be validated due to technical infeasibility

analogy.explanation: Just as the model proposes combinatorial knockouts that exceed current lab capabilities, current XAI methods (like SHAP) produce explanations that look plausible but may not reflect true causal mechanisms—because they weren’t designed with the constraints of biological interpretability in mind. In both cases, the system outputs something that seems optimal internally but fails to align with the real-world validation framework: one ignores assay engineering limits, the other ignores explanatory depth needed for causal inference.

scenario.inputs: A CellForge prediction that gene X is a top regulator of phenotype Y, accompanied by a SHAP explanation highlighting features Z1–Z3 as most important
scenario.outputs: The researcher evaluates whether the SHAP values actually support a causal chain (e.g., does knocking out Z1 truly disrupt the pathway?) or merely reflect correlation captured by the model
scenario.edge_cases: When SHAP highlights a non-coding RNA with no known functional assay—similar to the expert’s problem of unassayable genes—the explanation appears informative but can’t be experimentally verified, revealing a gap between statistical attribution and biological sufficiency

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Identify usability obstacles in existing AI tools

conflict.expert_branch: It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify usability obstacles in existing AI tools

probes.question: Is your concern more tied to the model ignoring lab capacity signals or to it overlooking historical validation failure patterns?
Which matters more in your frustration: maximizing discovery yield or minimizing wasted experimental effort?
Does the issue stem more from the model outputting too many candidates or from the fixed monthly testing cap of ~20?
Is your concern related to overconfident predictions or to poorly discriminated confidence scores among top candidates?
choices: Poorly discriminated confidence scores among top candidates

probes.question: Which weighs more heavily: missing potentially high-impact genes outside the top 20, or repeating failed experiments due to false positives?
choices: Missing potentially high-impact genes outside the top 20
Repeating failed experiments due to false positives

probes.question: Is your concern more about small input changes causing large ranking shifts or about inconsistent ordering across model versions?
choices: Small input changes causing large ranking shifts

probes.question: Does your frustration arise more from the time cost per validation or from the reagent/resource cost per test?
Which threshold do you implicitly rely on more: a hard confidence cutoff or a relative rank within the top N?
choices: Relative rank within the top N

hypothetical_scenarios: If the model’s top prediction list was reduced from 100 to 20 high-confidence hits per run, would your judgment about 'It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck' change?

If the confidence scores were recalibrated so that only ~5% of predictions exceeded your current selection threshold (instead of 30%), would your judgment about 'It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck' change?
If your lab’s monthly testing capacity increased from 20 to 100 samples, would your judgment about 'It feels like the model doesn’t respect our screening bottleneck' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Poor performance on non-coding RNAs and poorly annotated genes
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Poor performance on non-coding RNAs and poorly annotated genes
conflict.researcher_branch: Assess confidence interpretation needs

analogy.source_concept: The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene perturbation inputs that, in reality, affect specific transcript isoforms but are represented as single-gene-level interventions
structural_mapping.logic: Model aggregates all biological complexity into a single gene-level representation, discarding known regulatory nuance
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that appear plausible for well-annotated genes but fail silently when isoform context matters

analogy.explanation: Just as the model’s monolithic gene representation hides critical isoform-level biology—leading experts to distrust its outputs when splicing is relevant—the lack of interpretable confidence metrics hides whether a prediction for a poorly annotated lncRNA is grounded in data or extrapolation. In both cases, the model presents a smooth, confident output that masks underlying ignorance, and experts need visibility into that uncertainty to judge reliability.

scenario.inputs: Perturbation of LINC01116 (a primate-specific lncRNA with minimal functional annotation) in a human iPSC-derived neuron under baseline culture conditions
scenario.outputs: A confidence score or uncertainty interval accompanying the predicted expression changes of downstream genes, explicitly indicating low reliability due to absence of LINC01116 in training data and weak homology signals
scenario.edge_cases: When the model assigns high confidence to a lncRNA perturbation because it co-expresses with a well-studied protein-coding gene, even though no causal link exists—expert would reject this as confounding, while the researcher’s method might still treat the co-expression pattern as sufficient evidence for confidence

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

conflict.expert_branch: Underrepresentation of stress-response conditions
conflict.researcher_branch: Evaluate sufficiency of current XAI methods

analogy.source_concept: The model seems blind to isoform-specific effects

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene-level perturbation data that collapses all isoforms into a single unit, ignoring splice variants
structural_mapping.logic: Model assumes uniform gene behavior regardless of context-dependent molecular forms, leading to oversimplified causal inferences
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that appear plausible under standard conditions but fail when isoform switching is biologically critical (e.g., during stress or differentiation)

analogy.explanation: Just as the model’s inability to distinguish isoforms leads to biologically implausible predictions under specific cellular states, current XAI methods may produce explanations that look reasonable in homeostatic settings but lack the granularity needed to trace causal regulatory logic under stress-response conditions. In both cases, the system’s internal representation omits context-sensitive biological nuance—isoforms for the expert, and dynamic regulatory dependencies for the researcher—resulting in outputs that are technically consistent but mechanistically misleading.

scenario.inputs: A CRISPRi knockdown of HIF1A in a virtual cell trained mostly on normoxic data, with the researcher requesting a SHAP-based explanation for the predicted expression changes in downstream targets like VEGFA and BNIP3
scenario.outputs: SHAP highlights strong feature importance for HIF1A and known co-factors, but fails to indicate that the explanation is unreliable because the model was never exposed to hypoxia-induced chromatin remodeling or HIF1A-ARNT dimerization dynamics
scenario.edge_cases: When the same perturbation is applied under simulated hypoxia—a condition absent from training—the model still generates a confident SHAP explanation, but the expert knows this scenario involves non-linear, context-dependent interactions (e.g., oxygen-sensitive degradation domains) that neither the model nor the XAI method can capture, creating a false sense of interpretability

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Batch effects from different CRISPR libraries propagate into predictions
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)

conflict.expert_branch: Batch effects from different CRISPR libraries propagate into predictions
conflict.researcher_branch: Document end-to-end manual perturbation workflow

analogy.source_concept: Off-target effects in training data create false dependencies

structural_mapping.inputs: Raw perturbation data containing unintended guide RNA interactions (off-targets) that masquerade as true biological signals
structural_mapping.logic: The model conflates spurious correlations with causal relationships because the training data doesn’t distinguish between on-target and off-target activity
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that reflect technical artifacts rather than genuine gene function

analogy.explanation: Documenting the end-to-end manual perturbation workflow is like identifying sources of off-target effects: both involve tracing how hidden, non-biological variables—whether experimental design choices or unintended molecular interactions—get baked into data. Just as off-target effects mislead models by introducing false dependencies, undocumented workflow steps (e.g., library selection, timing of harvest, or reagent batches) embed unaccounted biases that later appear as 'biological' signals in AI predictions. Capturing the full manual workflow reveals these latent confounders before they corrupt model learning.

scenario.inputs: A lab performs a CRISPRi screen using two different sgRNA libraries (e.g., Brunello vs. GeCKO) on the same cell line, with identical protocols except for the library used. The raw data shows divergent gene effect sizes for the same target gene across libraries.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s documentation effort would produce a step-by-step map showing when and how library choice was made, what QC metrics were applied per library, and how analysts decided which guides to trust—highlighting that library-specific guide efficiency differences, not biology, drive the divergence.
scenario.edge_cases: When the expert assumes batch correction alone can fix library-induced artifacts, but the researcher’s workflow documentation reveals that the libraries were designed with different promoter systems (e.g., U6 vs. H1), causing systematic expression differences that no post-hoc normalization can fully resolve—exposing a root cause invisible to purely data-driven correction.

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Off-target effects in training data create false dependencies
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Determine data inputs and outputs in practice

conflict.expert_branch: Off-target effects in training data create false dependencies
conflict.researcher_branch: Determine data inputs and outputs in practice

analogy.source_concept: Batch effects from different CRISPR libraries propagate into predictions

structural_mapping.inputs: Different CRISPR libraries (e.g., Brunello vs. GeCKO) with distinct guide RNA efficiencies and off-target profiles, treated as interchangeable in training data
structural_mapping.logic: The model conflates library-specific technical artifacts with true biological signal because it lacks explicit correction for source-specific biases
structural_mapping.outputs: Predictions that appear biologically plausible but are actually driven by which library was used in the training example

analogy.explanation: Just as batch effects from CRISPR libraries cause the model to learn spurious associations tied to technical origin rather than biology, the researcher’s focus on practical data inputs and outputs reveals how unstandardized input formats (e.g., inconsistent metadata about perturbation reagents) can silently encode similar confounders. If the model ingests perturbation data without knowing whether a guide came from Library A or B—or whether it had known off-target activity—it will treat all inputs as equivalent, reinforcing false dependencies. Understanding real-world input specifications (like reagent IDs, concentration, delivery method) is thus essential to prevent the model from learning artifacts disguised as biology.

scenario.inputs: A training dataset containing single-cell RNA-seq profiles from a Perturb-seq experiment where KIF11 was knocked down using two different sgRNAs: one with high on-target efficiency (sgKIF11-1) and another known to also suppress PLK1 (sgKIF11-2, off-target). The dataset includes raw counts, sgRNA assignments, and basic metadata (cell line, timepoint), but no annotation of off-target potential.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s workflow analysis would produce a specification document listing required input fields (e.g., sgRNA sequence, predicted off-target scores, library source) and expected output structure (e.g., normalized gene expression matrix with perturbation labels and confidence flags). This enables preprocessing steps that flag or exclude sgKIF11-2 unless its off-target effect is explicitly modeled.
scenario.edge_cases: When the same gene is perturbed by multiple reagents with divergent off-target profiles, the expert assumes the model should infer true biological effect by consensus, while the researcher insists the input schema must explicitly declare each reagent’s off-target risk so the model doesn’t treat them as replicates. Without this, the model learns a hybrid signal—part KIF11, part PLK1—as if it were a unified pathway dependency.

probes.question: When evaluating whether a pattern reflects real biology, do you rely more on consistency with known pathways or on statistical strength in the data?
Which aspect of the training data most undermines your confidence: sample size limitations or uneven representation across biological conditions?
Is your uncertainty about learned biology versus dataset quirks driven more by overrepresentation of high-impact findings or by underrepresentation of negative results?
Do you require mechanistic interpretability or just functional coherence to accept a pattern as biologically real?
Does your skepticism increase more when technical noise dominates or when biological variability is high?
Which gives you more confidence: replication in a similar dataset or validation in a methodologically distinct one?
choices: Validation in a methodologically distinct one

probes.question: Are you more concerned that patterns might be outdated due to evolving experimental protocols or due to shifting biological understanding?
When experts disagree on a pattern’s validity, does that make you suspect data artifacts or genuine biological complexity?

hypothetical_scenarios: If the training data included equal numbers of positive and negative results (eliminating publication bias), would your judgment about 'I can’t tell if it’s learned biology or dataset quirks' change?

If a predicted gene perturbation effect was replicated in three orthogonal experimental systems but lacked a known mechanism, would your judgment about 'I can’t tell if it’s learned biology or dataset quirks' change?

If the noise-to-signal ratio in RNA-seq measurements improved by an order of magnitude while keeping all other data characteristics fixed, would your judgment about 'I can’t tell if it’s learned biology or dataset quirks' change?

aligned_path_expert: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Minor input formatting changes cause large output swings
aligned_path_researcher: Virtual cell AI models for gene perturbation experiments (CellForge)
Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

conflict.expert_branch: Minor input formatting changes cause large output swings
conflict.researcher_branch: Catalog key research objectives for using CellForge

analogy.source_concept: It feels unstable—like it’s memorizing, not generalizing

structural_mapping.inputs: Gene identifiers presented in slightly different but biologically equivalent formats (e.g., Ensembl vs. HGNC)
structural_mapping.logic: The model treats superficial syntactic differences as meaningful biological signals rather than recognizing semantic equivalence
structural_mapping.outputs: Drastically different perturbation predictions for the same underlying biological target

analogy.explanation: Just as the expert worries the model memorizes training data instead of learning generalizable biological rules, cataloging research objectives requires distinguishing surface-level goals (e.g., 'find any gene') from deep scientific intent (e.g., 'identify causal drivers'). If the researcher’s objective catalog doesn’t account for semantic equivalence in goal formulation—like treating 'predict phenotype' and 'infer mechanism' as interchangeable—it risks producing unstable or misaligned experimental designs, mirroring how input formatting instability leads to unreliable predictions.

scenario.inputs: A researcher submits a query using HGNC symbol 'TP53' to identify therapeutic targets in glioblastoma; separately, another team submits the same biological question using Ensembl ID 'ENSG00000141510' for the same gene.
scenario.outputs: The researcher’s objective catalog would ideally map both inputs to the same high-level goal: 'discover therapeutic targets via perturbation of tumor suppressor genes in glioblastoma'. This enables consistent experimental design and interpretation across teams.
scenario.edge_cases: If the objective catalog treats 'therapeutic target discovery' and 'driver gene identification' as distinct without clarifying their overlap in this context, then minor phrasing differences in how researchers articulate goals—like swapping 'identify' for 'discover'—could lead to divergent CellForge configurations, analogous to how Ensembl vs. HGNC inputs trigger inconsistent model outputs.
